quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous vs. average rate"?

options.text: To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du
Average: Δf/Δx over [a,b]; instantaneous: f'(a) = lim(Δx→0) Δf/Δx at a single point
Numerical approximation: start at (x₀,y₀), step by yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + f(xₙ,yₙ)·Δx with step size Δx

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous vs. average rate" is defined as: Average: Δf/Δx over [a,b]; instantaneous: f'(a) = lim(Δx→0) Δf/Δx at a single point. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tangent line slope"?

options.text: Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
All variables are functions of time, so d/dt[x²] = 2x·dx/dt, d/dt[xy] = x·dy/dt + y·dx/dt
f'(a) equals the slope of the tangent to y = f(x) at x = a; the best linear approximation at that point

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tangent line slope" is defined as: f'(a) equals the slope of the tangent to y = f(x) at x = a; the best linear approximation at that point. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Units of derivative"?

options.text: Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C
In economics: marginal cost C'(x), marginal revenue R'(x), and marginal profit P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x) at production level x
The derivative f'(x) has units of [f-units]/[x-units]; velocity = m/s, marginal cost = $/unit

quiz_questions.explanation: "Units of derivative" is defined as: The derivative f'(x) has units of [f-units]/[x-units]; velocity = m/s, marginal cost = $/unit. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interpreting sign"?

A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interpreting sign" is defined as: f'(a) > 0: f is increasing at x = a; f'(a) < 0: f is decreasing; f'(a) = 0: f has a horizontal tangent. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interpreting magnitude"?

A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
Reading the y-value a function approaches by tracing the curve from both sides toward x = c; the function need not be defined at c
lim[f ± g] = lim f ± lim g; lim[f·g] = lim f · lim g; lim[f/g] = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0); lim[fⁿ] = (lim f)ⁿ

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interpreting magnitude" is defined as: |f'(a)| measures how rapidly f is changing: large |f'(a)| = steep curve, small |f'(a)| = nearly flat. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change?

options.text: Speedometers measure instantaneous velocity v(t) = s'(t), not average velocity—critical for traffic law enforcement and vehicle safety systems
Engineering error analysis: propagation of measurement uncertainty through formulas using differentials to estimate how input errors affect computed results
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Instantaneous Rate of Change?

options.text: Population modeling: if the growth rate dP/dt = f(t) is known, integrating gives the population P(t) = ∫f(t)dt + P₀
Heart rate monitors: the instantaneous rate of heartbeats per minute (derivative of cumulative beat count) triggers medical alerts when it exceeds thresholds
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Instantaneous Rate of Change applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Stock trading algorithms measuring the instantaneous rate of price change to trigger buy/sell orders in millisecond timeframes
Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Instantaneous Rate of Change?

options.text: Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point
Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
Speedometers measure instantaneous velocity v(t) = s'(t), not average velocity—critical for traffic law enforcement and vehicle safety systems
Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Instantaneous Rate of Change in practice?

options.text: Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Economics: ∫₀ᵀ (revenue rate - cost rate)dt = total profit over the period [0,T]
Heart rate monitors: the instantaneous rate of heartbeats per minute (derivative of cumulative beat count) triggers medical alerts when it exceeds thresholds
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Instantaneous Rate of Change.
